An investigation into the effect of levels of processing on

An investigation into the effect of levels of processing on

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and relevant aim with practical application to pre-university study strategies
Accurate description of levels-of-processing theory and historical context
Explicit connection between theory and investigation
Well-formulated hypotheses with correctly operationalised IV and DV
weaknesses: Occasional imprecise phrasing (e.g., âefficient resultsâ) weakens clarity
Hypotheses are non-directional rather than explicitly predicting deeper processing superiority
Linkage of each processing level to the aim could be more explicitly detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of the introduction meet the top band (5â6) descriptors but exhibit minor lapses in precision and depth. The aim is clearly stated and its relevance explained; the theory is accurately summarised and linked; variables are operationalised. Small wording imprecisions and non-directional hypothesis statements justify a low top-band score of 5 rather than a perfect 6.

strengths: Independent-measures design is chosen and justified to reduce demand characteristics
Participant characteristics and opportunity sampling rationale are clearly described
Multiple controlled variables and materials are listed with brief rationales
weaknesses: Some controlled variables (lighting, timing) lack explanation
Procedure omits details on how timing was standardised for recall
Justification for opportunity sampling could further address potential biases and generalisability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates an explained research design (band 3â4), justified sampling (band 4), and described controls (band 3). Uneven depth in variable explanations and procedural omissions place this holistically in the mid-band, meriting a score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics (mean, SD, Mann-Whitney U)
Bar graph directly addresses the hypothesis with correct format and labels
Findings are interpreted in relation to the data and hypothesis, linking deeper processing to improved recall
weaknesses: Statistical description contains errors (data called âratioâ, inconsistent means reported)
Graph lacks error bars and has cramped axis labeling
No effect size reported to convey practical significance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical and graphical methods are applied appropriately but contain errors (band 3â4), while interpretation fully links findings to the hypothesis (band 5â6). Overall, this sits in the mid-high range of the analysis criterion, justifying a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Discussion explicitly references the levels-of-processing theory and Craik & Tulving findings
Strengths and limitations are explained with clear links to validity and generalisability
Modifications are explicitly tied to limitations and thoroughly justified
weaknesses: Could expand discussion of external validity beyond gender balance
No mention of pilot testing to validate materials and procedures
Follow-up study proposals lack specificity regarding variables to manipulate
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All evaluation strands meet the highest band descriptors (5â6) with explicit theoretical linkage, justified modifications, and relevant strengths/limitations. Some areas could be deepened (external validity, pilot testing, targeted follow-up), placing this at a low top-band score of 5 rather than a perfect 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar graph correctly compares average recall across conditions and is labelled, directly addressing the hypothesis.  Minor issues (no error bars, cramped y-axis label) constitute âerrorsâ, so the work fits band 3-4 with a score of 3.

Findings are **clearly interpreted**: the student explains what the U value signifies, states rejection of the null, and links the statistical outcome to deeper processing improving recall.  Despite the earlier mean inconsistency, the interpretation relative to hypothesis is solid; hence low top-band (5).

Both null and research hypotheses are written; IV (type of processing: deep vs shallow) and DV (number of words recalled out of 20) are **correctly identified and operationalised** through question format and recall count.  Although phrasing is slightly repetitive, all operational details are present; therefore, low top-band (5) is justified.

Opportunity sampling is defined and the **choice is justified** by time/resource constraints; participant characteristics (non-psychology students, English proficiency) are detailed.  This moves beyond simple description, so the strand sits at the top of the 3-4 band (score 4).

Levels-of-processing theory is **accurately described** (structural, phonemic, semantic; depth definition) and the historical study by Craik & Tulving is summarised.  The student then **links the theory to the present investigation**, explicitly stating that deeper processing should improve recall and that the experiment tests this in students.  The linkage is clear, albeit brief, so a score of 5 within the 5-6 band is appropriate.

The discussion explicitly relates results to Craik & Tulving and the levels-of-processing framework, noting consistency with theory and specific error patterns.  Analysis goes beyond restating findings, showing conceptual linkage, so a score of 5 in the 5-6 band is justified.

The student clearly specifies the aim â to determine whether deep or shallow processing produces better recall in pre-university students â and then **explains its practical relevance** to study strategies for impending examinations.  The explanation is concise but explicit, showing why the investigation matters to the defined population, which aligns with the top-band descriptor.  Minor wording lapses (e.g., âstudy methods produce more efficient resultsâ) slightly weaken precision, so the strand is best placed low within the 5-6 band (score 5).

Strengths (non-psychology participants; reduced fatigue) and limitations (gender imbalance; English proficiency; seating) are **explained and clearly linked to validity or generalisability**, fulfilling the highest descriptor.  Some points could be deeper, so a 5, not 6, is awarded.

Several controlled variables (word order, font, seating, no talking) and key materials (A2 word list, PowerPoints) are listed with brief rationales, but some explanations are thin and a few controls (lighting, time of day) are omitted.  This meets the âexplainedâ criterion, yet with uneven depth, so a mid-band score of 3 is most fitting.

Proposed changes (stratified sampling, larger classroom, matched-pairs by English level) are **explicitly tied to the stated limitations** and show logical justification.  The suggestions are realistic and thoroughly reasoned, meriting a score of 5 in the top band.

The student names an independent-measures design, describes random allocation and **explains why this choice reduces demand characteristics**, fulfilling the âexplainedâ requirement.  Explanation is succinct but accurate, meriting the upper mark (4) in the 3-4 band.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) methods are used; however, the report later states contradictory means (13.6 vs 7.4) and incorrectly calls the data âratioâ.  These inaccuracies match the descriptor âapplied but contain errorsâ, warranting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Data are correctly summarised with mean and SD, but calling the data âratioâ is unnecessary; simply state they are quantitative scores.
The evaluation discussion successfully connects results to the levels-of-processing framework, demonstrating sound theoretical integration.
Strengths and limitations are well explained with links to validity and generalisability; consider expanding on external validity beyond gender balance.
Proposed modifications are explicitly tied to limitations and justified; including pilot testing of materials would further enhance methodological rigour.

The introduction effectively summarises levels-of-processing theory but could more explicitly connect each processing level description to your investigationâs aim to strengthen theoretical linkage.
Math notation \mathbb{L}^{\mathbb{N}} in the controlled variables section appears out of context; replace with clear description of seating distance.
Table 1 is clear but would benefit from reporting an effect size (e.g., r or Î·Â²) to convey the practical significance of the difference.
The bar graph addresses the hypothesis but lacks error bars to indicate variability; adding these would strengthen data presentation.
Interpretation correctly links U value to hypothesis, but the report of means (13.6 vs 7.4) contradicts earlier descriptive stats; verify consistency of values.
Citations to webpages lack author credentials; consider supplementing with primary sources or peer-reviewed articles to increase theoretical credibility.

The aim is clearly stated and relevance to pre-university students addressed; strengthening precision in phrasing (e.g., replace âefficient resultsâ with specific learning outcomes) would improve clarity.
Independent and dependent variables are identified and operationalised well; consider clarifying exactly how âdeepâ and âshallowâ tasks are counterbalanced to reduce order effects.
The null and experimental hypotheses match but are non-directional; revising the experimental hypothesis to explicitly predict deeper processing will enhance specificity.
Justification for using an independent measures design is well linked to demand characteristics; consider acknowledging potential participant variability as a trade-off.
Opportunity sampling is described and justified by constraints; expand on how this method might bias results and affect generalisability.
Participantsâ English proficiency is noted as a control; consider validating proficiency level with a brief pre-test to ensure consistency.
The procedure section details steps clearly but omits mention of timing controls for the recall phase; include how timing was standardized across participants.
Controlled variables are listed but explanations for some (e.g., lighting, noise levels) are missing; expand rationale to demonstrate thorough control.
The conclusion restates findings and significance but could propose a more targeted follow-up study specifying which variables to manipulate next.
Future research suggestion exploring gender differences is insightful; clarify whether this is exploratory or hypothesis-driven to align with IB expectations.
